title: Buying weaners: selection and transport
subtitle: Choosing healthy eight-week-old pigs, loading them safely, and observing the 20-day standstill.
excerpt: A bad buying decision is the hardest mistake to recover from. The cost of a weaner is small compared to 16 weeks of feed, so spending time on selection is worthwhile. Know the standstill rules before you travel: moving pigs onto your holding triggers a legal 20-day restriction on moving other pigs off.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Guidance on pig identification and movement rules. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/pig-identification-and-movement
AHDB Pork, Buying pigs and biosecurity. https://ahdb.org.uk
Pig Veterinary Society, Weaner health guidelines. https://www.pigvetsoc.org.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: A young pig weaned from its mother, typically at four to eight weeks old and weighing 6 to 10 kg at purchase. Weaners sold at eight weeks are more robust than those sold at four weeks and are the standard starting point for small-scale pig keepers.

A legal restriction that prevents the movement of pigs off a holding for 20 days after pigs arrive on that holding. The standstill applies regardless of whether the incoming and outgoing pigs are in contact. It is enforced under the Pigs (Records, Identification and Movement) Order 2011.

County Parish Holding number: a nine-digit number (formatted as county/parish/holding) issued by the Rural Payments Agency. A CPH number is required before pigs can be kept or moved legally. It identifies the physical location of the holding.

Electronic Animal Movement Licence 2: the online system used in England and Wales to record pig movements between holdings. Both the seller and buyer must record the movement in eAML2 within three days of the pigs moving.

. Apply via the Rural Payments Agency before you buy. Once pigs arrive, the 20-day
What to look for at the source
Visit the seller's holding if possible. A healthy

at eight weeks should weigh between 8 and 12 kg. It should move without stiffness. Eyes should be clear with no discharge. The nose should be damp but not streaming. Skin should be smooth with no raised patches, sores, or mange crusting.

Watch the group at rest and at the feeder. Pigs that compete normally at the trough are alert and healthy. A weaner that hangs back or lies alone when the others are active is a risk. Check the dung: loose or watery scour is a red flag. The pen should smell of pig, not of ammonia or necrosis.

If you are buying two or more pigs, select from the same litter where possible. Pigs that are familiar with each other settle faster and fight less. A large size difference within a group causes problems: the smaller pig is pushed off feed and rarely catches up. Aim for pigs within 1 kg of each other.

Weaners must travel in a clean, ventilated, escape-proof container. A solid-sided box with ventilation slats works well for short journeys. Line the floor with straw so the pigs have grip. The journey should be as short as possible: weaners are susceptible to chilling and stress in transit.

Do not mix pigs from different sources in the same transport box. Stress and unfamiliar animals together trigger fighting injuries. Load calmly, avoid shouting, and use a pig board to guide rather than push.

attrs.title: 20-day standstill
attrs.body: From the moment pigs arrive on your holding, you cannot legally move any pig off the holding for 20 days. This applies even if the incoming and outgoing pigs never share a field. Plan your buying date so the standstill does not block a planned slaughter movement. The restriction is on movement off the holding; you can still move pigs between fields on the same CPH during a standstill.

content.text: The seller must provide a movement licence from eAML2. Check it matches the number of pigs, their eartag numbers, the seller's CPH, and your CPH. Both parties must record the movement within three days. Keep the paperwork for at least three years. If the pigs are already eartag-identified, record those tags in your holding records on arrival.
